pubmed_links: [Futamura et al. (2002) Abnormal expression of epidermal growth factor and its receptor in the forebrain and serum of schizophrenic patients](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12192610/); [Tohmi et al. (2003) Neonatal perturbation of neurotrophic signaling results in abnormal sensorimotor gating and social interaction in adults](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12556905/); [Streck et al. (2020) Serum Epidermal Growth Factor is Low in Schizophrenia and Not Affected by Antipsychotics Alone or Combined With Electroconvulsive Therapy](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32194452/)
summary: EGF protein and serum levels are consistently reduced in schizophrenia patients, and neonatal EGF signaling perturbation in animal models produces persistent dopaminergic dysregulation and schizophrenia-relevant behavioral deficits including impaired prepulse inhibition and social interaction. (emerging evidence)
